channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Fed Meeting Underscores Tough Task Ahead for Warsh
item.description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday announced he would stay on as a governor at the central bank as internal divisions sharpen about the policy path forward.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday said that he would remain at the central bank after his term as chair ends on May 15 and his successor, Kevin M. Warsh, takes over.

item.title: Supreme Court Strikes Down Louisiana Map, Another Blow to Voting Rights Act
item.description: The court struck down the voting map as an unconstitutional racial gerrymander in a move that could make it harder for lawmakers to create majority-minority voting districts.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday.

item.title: What the Supreme Court’s Voting Rights Ruling Could Mean for the Midterms
item.description: Democrats stand to lose at least one blue-leaning district in Louisiana, but the timing was unclear. Florida has approved a redder map, and Republicans in several other states are weighing new districts.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Emily Cochrane and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Marsha Blackburn of Tennessee issued a statement calling on the state legislature to reconvene and pass a new map that eliminates a majority-Black district in Memphis.

item.title: Civil rights leaders see the voting rights ruling as a ‘betrayal.’
item.description: Leaders and activists said a Supreme Court ruling had the potential to unravel decades of work toward giving Black Americans fair representation.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch
item.media:credit: Maansi Srivastava for The New York Times
item.media:description: “To be honest, this feels like a betrayal,” said Marc Morial, president of the Urban League.

item.title: British Royals Crisscross Manhattan in Brief Visit Packed With Photo Ops
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the Sept. 11 memorial before stopping by an urban farm, the New York Public Library, a business event and a gala.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear, Claire Fahy and Sarah Lyall
item.category: Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Bloomberg, left, King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the 9/11 memorial on Wednesday morning, the first of several stops during the royal couple’s visit to New York City.

item.title: What the Royal State Dinner Guest List Says About Trump’s America
item.description: There were at least 10 American billionaires, six Fox News hosts, assorted presidential pals, no Democratic politicians and not so many British.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Bumiller
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Melania Trump with King Charles III of Britain and Queen Camilla at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: Mamdani’s Advice to Royals: Give That Priceless Diamond Back to India
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani and King Charles did not meet privately. But if they had, the mayor said, he probably would have raised the issue of the Koh-i-Noor Diamond.
item.dc:creator: Jeffery C. Mays
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Jeenah Moon
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York speaking with King Charles III at the 9/11 memorial on Wednesday.

item.title: Queen Camilla Unites Winnie-the-Pooh With a Long-Lost Friend
item.description: On Wednesday, the Queen of England presented the New York Public Library with a bespoke replica of Roo, the smallest companion of the Bear of Very Little Brain.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall
item.category: Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Chris Jackson
item.media:description: Queen Camilla presents a specially made replica of Roo, the baby kangaroo companion of Winnie-the-Pooh, to Anthony Marx, the president and chief executive of the New York Public Library.

item.title: Pentagon Puts Iran War Cost at $25 Billion as Hegseth Berates Skeptics
item.description: During his first public appearance on Capitol Hill since the war began, the defense secretary lashed out at lawmakers in both parties who have questioned the conflict.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, during a hearing at the Capitol on Wednesday.

item.title: Takeaways From Hegseth’s Testimony on Iran War and His Tenure
item.description: It was Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s first public appearance before Congress since Operation Epic Fury began in late February.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay and Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine testified during a congressional hearing on Wednesday.

item.title: Hegseth Cites Falsehood to Defend His Firing of Senior Officers
item.description: The defense secretary said at a House hearing that President Barack Obama had fired 197 generals, a figure that the Pentagon previously acknowledged was false.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s actions to fire senior military leaders are without precedent in recent decades and have come with little explanation.

item.title: Trump Threatens to Pull Troops From Germany as He Lashes Out at Chancellor
item.description: The comments came after the German chancellor said that Iran has “humiliated” the United States.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Sean Gallup/Getty Images
item.media:description: U.S. soldiers on a combat exercise in Bavaria, Germany.

item.title: Lucinda Williams Explains What It Takes to Write a Great Song
item.description: The singer-songwriter talks about being self-taught, and reaching down into the deepest, darkest parts of herself to pull out a song.

item.title: How Taylor Swift Writes a Song, In Her Own Words
item.description: The artist shares stories behind some of her biggest hits, her love of a “rant bridge” and how life in the public eye informs the stories she tells in her songs.

item.title: Rising Fuel Prices Could Force Excruciating Choices on Economic Policies
item.description: The European Central Bank and Bank of England were expected to hold interest rates steady on Thursday while searching for signs of possible longer-term damage.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Paris. The war in Iran had an almost immediate impact on European inflation, increasing gasoline prices as well as airfares and other fuel-intensive activities.

item.title: Oil Prices Soar as Iran Standoff Shows No End in Sight
item.description: Oil prices push higher, approaching another wartime high, as President Trump asserted that the naval blockade of Iran’s ports would persist.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images

item.title: House Adopts Budget to Unlock $70 Billion for Immigration Enforcement
item.description: The measure will allow the G.O.P. to begin working on a filibuster-proof bill to fund ICE and C.B.P., part of their plan to reopen the long-shuttered Department of Homeland Security.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents outside a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Chicago last year.

item.title: Surveillance Law Is in Limbo After House Approval
item.description: Republicans put down a right-wing revolt to push a three-year renewal through the House, but the Senate appeared likely to opt for a 45-day punt ahead of a Friday expiration.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage and Michael Gold
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson struggled for more than two hours on Wednesday to round up the votes to bring up the surveillance bill.

item.title: They Left for the School Bus. ICE Picked Them Up Instead.
item.description: A school transfer disrupted two brothers’ visas, their lawyer said, leaving them vulnerable to arrest and unsettling their Mississippi school community.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales and Hamed Aleaziz

item.title: G.O.P. Congress Struggles to Do the Basics Amid Party Infighting
item.description: House Republicans toiled to pass major measures as lawmakers tried to fund homeland security, extend spy powers and deliver a farm bill.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Right now we don’t look as functional as we need to look,” Representative Tom Cole, Republican of Oklahoma, acknowledged.

item.title: J. Craig Venter, Scientist Who Decoded the Human Genome, Dies at 79
item.description: A risk-taking outsider, he brought speed, competition and controversy to one of science’s biggest races.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Wade
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: J. Craig Venter, next to Algae growth in his greenhouse in La Jolla, Calif., competed with a rival team of scientists in assembling the first human genome.

item.title: In France, Trump’s Triumphal Arch Draws Eye Rolls
item.description: The Arc de Triomphe in Paris, which the president vows to top in Washington, offers a lesson in the complicated history of monuments.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conceived in 1806 by Napoleon, the Arc de Triomphe has come to symbolize very different things in modern France.

item.title: Lester Wright, the Fastest Known Centenarian, Dies at 103
item.description: In 2022, at 100, he set what was believed to be a world record in the 100-meter dash at the Penn Relays in Philadelphia, beating 86- and 92-year-old competitors.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: These Runners Kept Going to 100 or Beyond
item.description: Many picked up running late in life. They all shared an uncommon competitive drive.
item.dc:creator: Amisha Padnani
item.media:credit: Brit Huckabay/National Senior Games Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Julia Hawkins competing in the 50-meter dash at the National Senior Games in Albuquerque in 2019.

item.title: The Murky Ethics of Swimming With Killer Whales
item.description: Only two places in the world allow tourists to enter the water with the ocean’s apex predator. But the safety of both species is a growing concern.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Meghan Dhaliwal
item.media:credit: Meghan Dhaliwal for The New York Times

item.title: Musk Says He ‘Was a Fool’ to Provide OpenAI’s Early Funding
item.description: In the second day of a trial pitting Mr. Musk against OpenAI, he said the company’s chief executive, Sam Altman, had misled him. But OpenAI’s lawyer said evidence showed the opposite.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk’s testimony grew testy during cross-examination from OpenAI’s lawyer.

item.title: A.I. Spending Sets a Record, With No End in Sight
item.description: Google, Amazon, Microsoft and Meta reported more than $130 billion in quarterly capital expenditures on Wednesday as they build A.I. data centers. There’s more to come.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: AJ Mast for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amazon’s data center complex in New Carlisle, Ind. The company, along with Google, Microsoft and Meta, has spent billions on capital expenditures, largely data centers.

item.title: Meta Deal Reversal Deepens Split Between China and Silicon Valley
item.description: Beijing’s insistence that Meta unwind its deal with a Chinese A.I. start-up escalates the geopolitical fight over advanced tech.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: China demanded on Monday that Meta’s $2 billion deal with the A.I. start-up Manus be undone.

item.title: The Justices Acted as Partisans in the Voting Rights Ruling
item.description: In the name of disentangling race from politics, the court has given white voters more power at the expense of racial minorities.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Two Cocky Authoritarians Blocking Hormuz: What Could Go Wrong?
item.description: In the Iran war, we have two overconfident administrations facing off, each believing that time is on its side.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Atta Kenare/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicting an Iranian net catching American military planes.

item.title: Josh Johnson Gives King Charles Props for His Comedy Chops
item.description: “You know, I’ve always wondered what white Def Jam would be like,” Johnson said after King Charles cracked a few jokes at the White House.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: U.S. Indictment Accuses Mexican Governor of Conspiring With Sinaloa Cartel
item.description: Prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, and other Mexican officials of a yearslong conspiracy to protect the powerful cartel.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Maria Abi-Habib and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Rashide Frias/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, is the highest-ranking member of Mexico’s dominant political party, Morena, to be indicted by the United States.

item.title: D4vd Bought Chain Saws to Dispose of Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s Body, Prosecutors Say
item.description: In a legal filing, prosecutors outlined for the first time how they say the singer murdered and dismembered Celeste Rivas Hernandez.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens, Emmanuel Morgan and Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A neighbor of Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s at a memorial in Lake Elsinore, Calf.

item.title: Mississippi Middle School Students Avert Bus Crash After Driver Loses Control
item.description: Footage of the incident shared this week by a school district in Mississippi shows a group of students working together to avert disaster on a highway.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Hancock County School District, via Associated Press

item.title: Indian Tycoon Offers Refuge to Pablo Escobar’s Condemned Hippos
item.description: Colombia was planning to slaughter 80 hippopotamuses after a small herd imported in the 1980s by the drug lord grew out of control. An Indian tycoon has offered them a new home instead.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species
item.media:credit: Raul Arboleda/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Hippos descended from four imported by the drug kingpin Pablo Escobar in Colombia, in a 2023 photo.
